Why does somebody really come to a seminar? Well, on one level they come maybe because they're expected to, like it's the National Convention of us and so. Maybe they come on one level because of what the seminar is, who the speakers are, what's going on, etc. Maybe they come because of the fraternal aspect of the seminar. It's the place to see people you only see once a year and so forth. So there's a lot of feature benefit to the whole thing. But ultimately they're there because of certain, vaguer but more powerful aspirations. They're there because they want to be perceived in a certain way. They want to feel about themselves in a certain way. They want to be like the other people who are there or the people who are putting it on. In other words, the attendance of it symbolizes something to them. It represents something to them. Buying a product, car, book, these things symbolize something to people. And when you get at the value, the aspirational value of the thing, now it's easier to make the sale, it's easier to keep them interested in you if you're connected to those values. Price goes away. And maybe what's interesting about this is that people often enunciate a fairly pessimistic view of their business or their life or their activity, but deep down inside they're optimists. That's why we don't have an 80% suicide rate because they talk themselves out of it every single day. We have mortgage business. Well, no, even then. I mean, we've only had mass suicide in this country on one day, right? The day of the great crash, people actually were out there jumping off of ledges. People buy lottery tickets. People marry for the third time, the fourth time, the fifth time, the sixth time. I mean, so all around there's behavior that demonstrates this hopeful vein in people, regardless of what they say. And you want to connect to their hopefulness. You always want to be showing them something they can aspire to that is meaningful to them. So if you think about this thing, okay, and why you're here, okay, on a practical level, if you enunciated the practical things, you want to be able to write better, you want to be able to write faster, you have particular problems in your business you want to solve. I mean, all your questionnaires told me that, right? So some people, it was about attracting new customers more than it was anything else. Some people, it was about retention more than it was anything else. Some people, it was about the need to be faster and more prolific more than it was anything else, et cetera, et cetera, et cetera. But then the layer above that is certain aspirations are represented by this, right? So some of that is not so arrogant, but you voiced it in different ways, is we'd really like to be able to write more like Dan, right? So that's a hold this thing up and here you can be this. Some of it is really want to, your aspiration would be voicing, your aspiration would be voiced, the value would be being looked upon differently by the people that you work with than they look upon you now. You sort of perhaps envy's too strong a word, admire the influence I have over my troops. And the thought is I'd like to be able to have that kind of influence. So there's an aspirational level to this thing that separated you from your money, actually more so than the nuts and bolts of what are we going to cover and what are you going to walk away with. And now in the delivery, like you got to have eight or ten checklists or you wouldn't be happy. And so I understand that and you got to have dessert. You always want to remember that about seminars. It's one of the most important elements in satisfying the troops. You got to give them dessert. Pretty much you give crappy food but you got to give them dessert. But the aspirational level, the hopefulness of, you know, I'm going to leave this thing and be a more powerful or a more persuasive or more influential or what's difficult is going to be easier for me. You know, that's what you really got to try and connect to as part of what you write about to people. So here's a great ad example. So these are actual ads. So on page 194, here was the real estate ad as the real estate agent wrote it. And on page 195, you'll find the real estate ad as the consumer finally rewrote it after the house didn't sell. And so Bertha actually sat down and wrote her own ad about her house. And Bertha ought to be a professional copywriter because Bertha got it right. So take a minute and read the two ads. The differences, of course, are striking. That's exactly what it is. It's exactly what it is. I would go further. I would say it's a feelings and a values ad. And so what's important though is that it's not a thing ad. In fact, the house is almost incidental, right, to the ad. The connection, of course, has to be with somebody just like Bertha. So like this copy, it doesn't go sell this house to me. But the person who has this collection of values, it resonates with like there's no tomorrow. And it also, of course, makes price relatively unimportant. Certainly less important than it would be otherwise. And so the language used all the way down to the choice of home versus house. The language is all important. The structure is all important. But ultimately what she got right is operating on a level of aspirations for what we hope happens to us when we move into this new home and what our life there is going to be like. Not does the window view east or doesn't have a big bedroom or doesn't have a big bedroom, etc. Wouldn't you also say that this sets it up so when they actually arrive at the home she can talk to her and move out sitting by the fire main cozy instead of... Yeah. Now I don't know that Bertha was that astute. My guess is this was an intuitive accident. But...and we don't give enough credit, by the way, to people's intuition. But if Bertha was that astute, of course, it sets up an entirely different way now to conduct the entire presentation now once they arrive. And ideally it would be congruent all the way through. So the language Bertha would use... See now like if Bertha hands this ad to a real estate agent, the real estate agent now runs the better ad and a bunch of people show up and the agent now deals with them the same way the agent would normally deal with everybody. Now you've almost defeated the purpose of the ad. Right? So yeah, it sets up a certain thing now that should happen when we get there. On page...
